title: From memex to logex: Why Every session Deserves Its Own Paper
summary: How an afternoon brainstorm evolved from the intuition that "sessions should leave something behind," through naming iterations like engram and session-brain, to converge on logex—a system that transforms AI sessions into searchable, linkable technical articles.
body:
# From memex to logex: Why Every session Deserves Its Own Paper

One o'clock on the afternoon of April 13th, I stared at my memex Zettelkasten card collection and the infographics generated by session-recap, and suddenly felt something was wrong.

memex is great—it's my external brain, atomized knowledge cards, bidirectional links, easy retrieval. session-recap isn't bad either, churning out beautiful infographics after each session, crystal clear at a glance. But there's a massive blind spot between them: the complete narrative of the session itself.

A typical Claude Code session spans dozens of chunks and hundreds of messages, containing design decisions, debugging records, and trade-off discussions where we tear everything down and start over. memex extracts atomic knowledge—too fragmented, losing context. session-recap paints bird's-eye views—too coarse, missing the flesh and blood. What I need is that middle layer: a proper technical article with a complete narrative arc, documenting the entire journey from problem to solution.

## First Round: What Is This Thing Really

I fired up OPC's dream-works pipeline, bringing PM, architect, and the tenth person together to examine this idea. The core question surfaced quickly: is this a memex feature or a standalone product?

The answer is standalone product. The reason is simple—memex's design philosophy is "atomization": one card, one insight, permanently valid. session paper's design philosophy is "narrativization": one article, one story, with timeline, context, and emotion. They differ in granularity, consumption patterns, and lifecycle. A memex card might still be referenced five years later. A session paper is more like a technical diary—when you look back three months later, its value lies in "so that's how I was thinking at the time."

But they can share infrastructure. Bidirectional links are good. Tag systems are good. No need to reinvent the wheel.

## The Naming Wars: brain, engram, logex

Naming took more time than expected. "session-brain" was the first candidate—straightforward but too generic. The word "brain" is overused, from Second Brain to various AI brain products, drowning in noise on search engines.

Then engram appeared—neuroscience term for "memory trace." The engram a session leaves in your brain—a precise metaphor. I was set on this name for a while, even started writing the implementation plan.

But I switched in the end. engram's problems: 1) Most people don't recognize the word, requires explanation; 2) It implies passive memory storage, while I want active knowledge distillation. Final decision: logex—shorthand for log + extraction, while paying homage to memex (memory + extraction). Short, spellable, immediately suggests log extraction.

## Architecture Decision: flat files Win

The storage solution discussion was surprisingly quick. Three options: database (Supabase/SQLite), flat files + git repo, or support both.

I chose flat files + git repo as the default. Reasoning:

1. **Rich markdown with images is naturally git-friendly**. Articles are `.md` files, images in the same directory, git tracks change history. Obsidian users have already proven this path works.
2. **Zero-dependency deployment**. No database service needed, no extra infra costs. A GitHub repo is the complete data layer.
3. **Portability**. When switching tools, your data is just markdown files in a folder—no vendor lock-in.

Of course, for full-text search and multi-user collaboration scenarios, database solutions fit better. So logex is designed with storage adapter pattern—default git repo, optional Supabase. But v1 only does git. YAGNI.

## Key Design: Auto-Segmentation

This is the most interesting design point in the entire system. A session doesn't necessarily produce just one article. You might spend the first half designing OAuth flow and the second half tweaking CSS layout—these are two completely different stories. Forcing them into one article just makes both stories worse.

So logex needs topic segmentation: read the session's chunk summaries, identify topic transition boundaries, group consecutive related chunks together, and make each group a separate article. Grouping criteria include time intervals, topic keyword changes, and score distribution. chunks scoring below 0.25 are skipped entirely—those are usually boilerplate messages for context continuation or interruption recovery, with no narrative value.

This segmentation logic alone deserves its own paper. But v1's implementation is straightforward: mainly time intervals + keyword clustering, no fancy NLP. Good enough works.

## Retrospective: Concept Phase Efficiency

From 1 PM brainstorming to finishing the implementation plan in the evening—about six hours. Two context overflows caused session continuations, losing some discussion context (this is exactly the problem logex aims to solve—if we'd had logex then, auto-archiving before each continuation would have prevented the loss).

The biggest gain from this phase wasn't code, but a set of clear design constraints: independent from memex, flat files first, auto-segmentation, tags + bidirectional links. With these constraints, the subsequent implementation becomes much faster. Spend an extra hour in design, avoid three pitfalls in implementation—that math always works out.
